# eGeMAPS Feature Pruning for Depression Regression — Literature Review

**Purpose.** Reduce the 88-dim eGeMAPSv02 functionals vector (openSMILE output per wav file) to ≤52 features for participant-level PHQ-9 regression on MODMA (n=52, Mandarin). With 52 subjects and 88 features, at least 36 must be cut. This review identifies which features have empirical support for depression detection and which can be safely pruned, with citations.

**Scope.** Based on the 88 features actually present in the `*_openSMILE_func.csv` files generated by `scripts/extract_opensmile_features.py`.

---

## 1. Features with STRONG empirical evidence — RETAIN

These recur as top predictors across independent studies (English, German, Mandarin cohorts) for MDD vs HC classification or PHQ/HAMD severity regression.

- **F0 mean, percentiles (20/50/80), variability (stddevNorm, pctlrange0-2)** — Lowered F0 variability ("monotone speech") is the single most replicated prosodic marker of depression. Negative correlation between F0 SD and HAMD severity in women with MDD (Almaghrabi et al., 2023, *Front. Psychiatry*). Top-ranked in AVEC 2013/2014 baselines (Valstar et al., ACM MM). Eyben et al. 2016 (*IEEE TAC*) motivates stddevNorm / pctlrange over raw stddev for robustness.
- **F0 rising/falling slope (mean + stddev)** — Flattened F0 slope indexes psychomotor retardation (Yang et al. 2013, *IEEE TAC*, "Detecting Depression Severity from Vocal Prosody"; Mundt et al. 2012).
- **Loudness mean, percentiles, loudness slopes, loudnessPeaksPerSec** — Lowered intensity and dynamics are classic markers. Tasnim & Novikova (ICMLA 2022, Winterlight Labs) explicitly report their interpretable model "identifies reductions in loudness and F0 as relevant signals of depression." Also Cummins et al. 2015 (*Speech Communication* review).
- **Jitter (local) amean** — Positive correlation with HAMD (r = 0.39, p = 0.02) in Almaghrabi et al. 2023. Reported in Teixeira et al. 2013 (*Procedia*) and Low et al. 2020 (*Laryngoscope Investig. Otolaryngol.* systematic review).
- **Shimmer (localdB) amean** — Moderate, replicated marker (Low et al. 2020; Cummins 2015). Weaker than jitter but retained in most pipelines.
- **HNR (HNRdBACF) amean** — Flagged as the largest single-feature discriminator in a MODMA-validated hybrid-feature study (Jiang et al. 2024, *J. Affect. Disord.*). Moderate effect in Wang et al. (PMC9095973).
- **MFCC 1–4 amean + stddevNorm (full) and V (voiced) variants** — MFCCs dominated SHAP rankings across 5 classifiers in a Mandarin study on Chinese university students (Frontiers Public Health 2025, 10.3389/fpubh.2025.1561332) — directly transferable to MODMA. MFCC1–2 track spectral tilt and vocal effort; MFCC4 positively associated with depression (interpretable foundation-model work, arXiv 2406.03138).
- **spectralFlux amean + stddevNorm (full and V)** — Top contributor in Huang et al. 2023 (*Transl. Psychiatry*) and linked to psychomotor retardation via reduced spectral dynamics (Stasak et al., Interspeech 2017).
- **F1 and F2 frequency (amean + stddevNorm)** — Depressed speakers show reduced F2 (centralized vowels, articulatory undershoot). Flint et al. 1993 (*J. Psychiatr. Res.*), replicated by Scherer, Morency & Stratou on DAIC-WOZ.
- **MeanVoicedSegmentLengthSec, MeanUnvoicedSegmentLength, VoicedSegmentsPerSec** — Pause and voicing-rate features are the most robust timing correlates of psychomotor retardation. Mundt et al. 2012 (*J. Neurolinguistics*); Trevino, Quatieri & Malyska 2011 (Interspeech); PLOS One 2020 (10.1371/journal.pone.0238726). Confirmed in Ringeval et al. AVEC 2017/2019 baselines.
- **equivalentSoundLevel_dBp** — Encodes lowered overall vocal intensity; partially redundant with loudness_amean but commonly retained as a speaker-level summary.
- **alphaRatioV, hammarbergIndexV, slopeV0-500, slopeV500-1500 (amean)** — Voiced spectral-balance means index vocal effort / tenseness, consistently retained in AVEC baselines (Eyben et al. 2016).

---

## 2. Features with WEAK / INCONSISTENT / REDUNDANT evidence — PRUNE

- **All formant bandwidths: F1/F2/F3 bandwidth amean + stddevNorm (6 features).** Bandwidths capture formant sharpness and are highly sensitive to recording conditions and voicing estimation. Almost never in top-20 lists for depression ablations (Huang et al. 2023; Frontiers Public Health 2025). Cummins 2015 explicitly flags formant-bandwidth results as "inconsistent." **Drop all 6.**
- **F3 frequency (amean + stddevNorm), 2 features.** F3 tracks rhotic / lip-rounding effects not cleanly tied to depression. Flint 1993 emphasizes F1/F2 only. **Drop both.**
- **F1/F2/F3 amplitudeLogRelF0 amean + stddevNorm (6 features).** Formant amplitude functionals are noisy and highly correlated with overall loudness / spectral tilt. Not flagged in any depression ablation surveyed. **Drop all 6**, or keep only F1 amplitudeLogRelF0 amean as a vocal-effort proxy.
- **Unvoiced spectral features: alphaRatioUV, hammarbergIndexUV, slopeUV0-500, slopeUV500-1500, spectralFluxUV (5 features).** Unvoiced segments carry limited affect signal. Voiced variants dominate in AVEC baselines and Stasak et al. 2017. **Drop 4 of 5**; optionally retain spectralFluxUV if voiced-flux is already kept.
- **Jitter stddevNorm, shimmer stddevNorm, HNR stddevNorm (3 features).** Second-order statistics of already-noisy perturbation measures; high correlation with their amean counterparts (Low et al. 2020; Teixeira 2013). **Drop all 3; keep the ameans.**
- **logRelF0-H1-H2 amean + stddevNorm, logRelF0-H1-A3 amean + stddevNorm (4 features).** Theoretically motivated (breathiness, vocal effort) but empirically weak for depression in eGeMAPS-level ablations. No clean citation showing them at top of feature-importance lists for MDD. **Likely prunable — evidence is genuinely thin, flag as a judgment call.**
- **StddevVoicedSegmentLengthSec, StddevUnvoicedSegmentLength (2 features).** Second-order timing stats are noisier than means; means carry the signal (Trevino et al. 2011). **Drop both.**
- **slopeV0-500 stddevNorm, slopeV500-1500 stddevNorm, alphaRatioV stddevNorm, hammarbergIndexV stddevNorm (4 features).** Voiced spectral-slope / balance stddevs rarely appear as top predictors; ameans carry the signal. **Consider dropping** to buy budget.

---

## 3. Mandarin-specific considerations (tonal language)

- **F0 carries lexical tone in Mandarin**, so raw F0 amean is partially driven by tone distribution in the prompts, not purely by affect. Zhang et al., *PLOS One* 2023 (10.1371/journal.pone.0283635) show emotion still modulates Mandarin F0 acoustics, but duration and amplitude compensate more than in non-tonal languages. **Implication:** F0 variability (stddevNorm, pctlrange0-2) and slope features remain informative; F0 amean alone is weaker in Mandarin than English — do not over-weight it.
- **Amplitude and duration matter more in tonal languages** for affective signaling (Zhang 2023). This strengthens keeping loudness percentiles, loudness slopes, and voicing-rate / segment-length features.
- **MFCCs are a particularly safe bet in Mandarin** — dominated SHAP rankings across 5 classifiers in the Frontiers Public Health 2025 Chinese student study.
- **No published paper specifically ablates eGeMAPSv02 on MODMA.** The closest is Jiang et al. 2024 (MODMA hybrid handcrafted features), highlighting HNR as the single most discriminative feature. Treat as corroboration, not proof.

---

## 4. Suggested 52-feature retained set

Target: ~52 features. Suggested retain list (counts):

| Group | Features | Count |
|---|---|---|
| F0 prosody | amean, stddevNorm, pct20/50/80, pctlrange0-2, meanRisingSlope, stddevRisingSlope, meanFallingSlope, stddevFallingSlope | 10 |
| Loudness | amean, stddevNorm, pct20/50/80, pctlrange0-2, meanRisingSlope, stddevRisingSlope, meanFallingSlope, stddevFallingSlope | 10 |
| Spectral flux (full) | amean, stddevNorm | 2 |
| MFCC 1–4 (full) | amean + stddevNorm | 8 |
| MFCC 1V–4V (voiced) | amean + stddevNorm | 8 |
| Jitter | amean only | 1 |
| Shimmer | amean only | 1 |
| HNR | amean only | 1 |
| Formants F1/F2 | frequency amean + stddevNorm | 4 |
| Voiced spectral balance | alphaRatioV amean, hammarbergIndexV amean, slopeV0-500 amean, slopeV500-1500 amean, spectralFluxV amean | 5 |
| Temporal / rate | loudnessPeaksPerSec, VoicedSegmentsPerSec, MeanVoicedSegmentLengthSec, MeanUnvoicedSegmentLength, equivalentSoundLevel_dBp | 5 |
| **Total retained** |  | **55** |

To trim to 52, candidates for further cut: shimmer_amean, alphaRatioV_amean, hammarbergIndexV_amean, or 1–2 F0/loudness stddev-based percentile features. Decision can be deferred to nested-CV feature selection inside the LOSO loop.

Dropped (33): F3_freq ×2, all formant bandwidths ×6, all formant amplitudes ×6, jitter_stddevNorm, shimmer_stddevNorm, HNR_stddevNorm, H1-H2 ×2, H1-A3 ×2, unvoiced-spectral alphaRatioUV / hammarbergIndexUV / slopeUV0-500 / slopeUV500-1500 / spectralFluxUV ×5, StddevVoicedSegmentLengthSec, StddevUnvoicedSegmentLength.

---

## 5. Honest gaps in the evidence

- No published ablation of the full 88-dim eGeMAPSv02 set on MODMA. The Frontiers Public Health 2025 Chinese student study is the closest Mandarin ablation.
- Glottal features (H1-H2, H1-A3) are under-studied for MDD in eGeMAPS-specific work; pruning them is a judgment call, not a well-cited decision.
- Most feature-importance reports come from tree-based classifiers on DAIC-WOZ (English); transfer to Mandarin continuous PHQ-9 regression at n=52 is assumed, not proven.
- With n=52, any literature-based prune is a first-stage filter only. Downstream feature selection inside nested CV (per CLAUDE.md's LOSO protocol) is still required.

---

## 6. Sources

- Eyben et al. 2016, "The Geneva Minimalistic Acoustic Parameter Set (GeMAPS)," *IEEE Transactions on Affective Computing* — https://sail.usc.edu/publications/files/eyben-preprinttaffc-2015.pdf
- Cai et al. 2020, MODMA dataset — https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.09283
- Jiang et al. 2024, *J. Affective Disorders*, MODMA hybrid handcrafted features — https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0165032724012151
- Chinese university students acoustic-based ML, *Frontiers Public Health* 2025 — https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/public-health/articles/10.3389/fpubh.2025.1561332/full
- Huang et al. 2023, "Relative importance of speech features," *Translational Psychiatry* — https://www.nature.com/articles/s41398-023-02594-0
- Almaghrabi et al. 2023, Women and depression acoustic features — https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10715859/
- Wang et al., Vocal Acoustic Features (PMC9095973) — https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9095973/
- Stasak et al., Interspeech 2017, Elicitation Design — https://www.isca-archive.org/interspeech_2017/stasak17_interspeech.html
- Tasnim & Novikova, ICMLA 2022 — https://winterlightlabs.com/assets/publications/icmla_2022_depression.pdf
- Interpretable speech foundation model for depression, arXiv 2406.03138 — https://arxiv.org/html/2406.03138
- Timing-related speech features and depression, *PLOS One* 2020 — https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0238726
- Bio-acoustic features of depression review, *Biomed. Signal Process. Control* 2024 — https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1746809423004536
- Voice of depression, *BMC Psychiatry* 2024 — https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12888-024-06253-6
- Emotional tones and Mandarin tones, *PLOS One* 2023 — https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0283635
- Teixeira et al. 2013, Jitter/Shimmer/HNR — https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212017313002788
- Manifestation of depression vs speaker identity (PMC10333314) — https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10333314/
- Flint et al. 1993, Abnormal speech articulation in MDD — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8295162/
- Automated depression diagnosis systematic review, *npj Mental Health Research* 2023 — https://www.nature.com/articles/s44184-023-00040-z
- Cummins et al. 2015, "A review of depression and suicide risk assessment using speech analysis," *Speech Communication*
- Yang et al. 2013, "Detecting Depression Severity from Vocal Prosody," *IEEE Transactions on Affective Computing*
- Mundt et al. 2012, *J. Neurolinguistics*
- Trevino, Quatieri & Malyska 2011, Interspeech
- Low et al. 2020, "Automated assessment of psychiatric disorders using speech," *Laryngoscope Investig. Otolaryngol.* systematic review
- Ringeval et al. AVEC 2017 / 2019 baselines, ACM MM workshops
- Valstar et al. AVEC 2013 / 2014 baselines, ACM MM workshops
- Scherer, Morency & Stratou, DAIC-WOZ depression work
